← all editions

The Chef Bababoefoe

Origin story · slug: `chef-bababoefoe`

The orange Bababoefoe woke up hungry, which is the only proper way for a chef to wake up.

It had found a tall white hat — a toque, though it did not know the word yet — sitting on a mushroom like a little cloud. The moment it put the hat on, it knew three things it had never known before: that flour is patient, that butter is kind, and that the best meals are the ones you share. This is how every Chef Bababoefoe is born: not by learning to cook, but by putting on the hat and suddenly caring.

In its hidden pocket, the Chef Bababoefoe carried the smallest, crustiest, most perfect baguette in the world — no longer than a matchstick, still warm. It was not for eating. It was a starter, the way a musician keeps a tuning fork: a reminder of how good bread should smell, so that nothing it baked would ever fall short.

That morning the burrow smelled of nothing at all, which for a chef is an emergency. So the Chef Bababoefoe got to work. It ground a single oat into flour. It borrowed one dewdrop for water and one honey-crumb for sweetness. It kneaded and folded and waited — patiently, the way flour likes — until it had baked exactly one tiny, golden bun.

One bun. And outside the door, a whole burrow of little critters beginning to smell the morning and blink awake.

Now, a chef who has made only one bun and has a crowd to feed has a choice. A worried chef would hide the bun. But the Chef Bababoefoe was not worried; it was a chef.

It cut the bun into the thinnest, fairest slices its stubby paws could manage — so thin you could see the sunrise through them — and then it reached out its paw. Its paw was magnetic, as all Bababoefoe paws are, and it clasped the hand of the next critter, who clasped the next, until a warm boefoe-chain ran all the way down the table. Down the chain the slices went, paw to paw, and somehow — the way good food always does — one small bun was enough for everyone, with the crust saved for last and shared too.

"A recipe isn't finished on the plate," said the Chef Bababoefoe, brushing flour from its whiskers. "It's finished when everyone at the table is holding on."

Then it tucked the little baguette back into its pocket, patted it once for luck, and started thinking about lunch.

If you ever meet a Chef Bababoefoe, ask to see its pocket. There is always a baguette inside, still warm. Nobody knows how.

A Dutch-first ("Nederlands eerst") version of this story is available as an owner option; only the English text is written here.

Chef Bababoefoe · colorway: orange · keepsake: a tiny warm baguette

Bababoefoe · every edition carries its own story.
